title: Hearing Protection Act
plain_summary: This bill would make it easier to buy firearm silencers by removing special federal taxes and registration rules that currently apply to them. Instead of the current system, silencers would be treated more like regular guns when people buy them. The government would have to destroy records they currently keep about silencer owners. States would not be allowed to create their own special taxes or registration rules for silencers, but there would be a new ten percent tax when silencers are made.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provis

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Hearing Protection Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Equal Treatment of Silencers and Firearms
sections.text: This section changes the tax code so that silencers are no longer treated as special firearms that need extra paperwork and taxes. It removes silencers from a list of weapons that have strict rules. This change will start ninety days after this law is passed.

sections.title: Section 3 Treatment of Certain Silencers
sections.text: If someone gets or owns a firearm silencer following the regular gun laws in Chapter forty-four of Title eighteen of the United States Code, they will be considered to have met all the registration and licensing requirements of the National Firearms Act for that silencer. This means they do not need to do extra paperwork just for the silencer.

sections.title: Section 4 Preemption of Certain State Laws in Relation to Firearm Silencers
sections.text: States and local governments cannot make laws that put special taxes on firearm silencers beyond normal sales taxes. They also cannot make laws requiring special marking, record keeping, or registration for silencers when those silencers are being made, sold, used, owned, or moved across state lines or to other countries. These state and local laws will have no power.

sections.title: Section 5 Destruction of Records
sections.text: Within one year after this law is passed, the Attorney General must destroy all government records about silencers. This includes any registration records for silencers kept in the National Firearms Registration and Transfer Record, any applications to transfer silencers, and any applications to make silencers.

sections.title: Section 6 Amendments to Title Eighteen United States Code
sections.text: This section updates the definitions and rules for firearm silencers and firearm mufflers in federal gun laws. It defines these devices as anything designed to make guns quieter when fired and that can attach to a gun directly or through mounting equipment. The section also requires gun dealers to follow the same age rules for selling silencers as they do for rifles and shotguns. For marking requirements, silencers must have serial numbers engraved on their main housing, but manufacturers can ask for permission to mark them differently if the normal way is not practical.

sections.title: Section 7 Imposition of Tax on Firearm Silencers or Firearm Mufflers
sections.text: This section adds firearm silencers and firearm mufflers to a list of items that have a ten percent tax when sold by manufacturers. It defines these devices as anything that makes guns quieter when fired. The section also updates other tax rules to include silencers and mufflers with other firearms and ammunition that are taxed. These tax changes will begin ninety days after this law is passed.
